identity.project_summary: An integrated, reproducible computational pipeline to model overall survival (OS) and progression-free survival (PFS) in ovarian cancer by combining multi-modal patient-level data. Integrates clinical labels (stage, drug combinations), genomic profiles (WES, HRD/HRP ratios, CNA status for CCNE1, MYC, MECOM from iMTB report), and multiplexed spatial proteomics features (derived from t-CycIF via SPACEstat). Employs survival models (Kaplan-Meier, Cox proportional hazards, Lasso regression), feature engineering (Variance Inflation Factor [VIF] redundancy checks, correlation heatmaps), and MOFA2 (Multi-Omics Factor Analysis) for unsupervised multi-modal integration. Spatial inputs include RCN proportions (RCN3, RCN9, RCN11), global/local immune-TSI distances, immune infiltration index (tumor vs stroma densities), nearest neighbor distance (NND), cluster density, and Gini density. Results are integrated directly with Project 12: SPACE.

**Supervisor: Anniina Färkkilä**

**Mentor: Ziqi Kang**

![](media/image2.png){width="6.5in" height="4.805555555555555in"}

## 

## 1. Background

Ovarian cancer is a heterogeneous disease with variable patient
outcomes. Identifying key clinical and experimental factors influencing
survival or the typing of clinical categories can improve treatment
personalization.

This project aims to apply survival analysis and different modeling
methods to assess the importance of features derived from:

> • **Clinical labels** - e.g., tumor stage, drug combinations, \...
>
> • **Genomic data** - e.g., copy number variation (CNV) from Whole
> exome sequencing (WES)
>
> • **Spatial data** - tumor/stroma ratio, immune infiltration,
> recurrence cellular neighborhood from tCyCIF
>
> ![](media/image1.png){width="4.175741469816273in"
> height="4.015904418197725in"}

## 2. Research Objectives

Before we list the objectives, we used the word survival models here.
However, based on Anniina\'s suggestion, we could start with the patient
categories of **HRD and HRP**, since we already know they have some
differences in tumor microenvironment.

- **Benchmarking and building survival models** (Cox
 > proportional-hazards model, Bayesian-based models, etc\...) to
 > assess the impact of clinical and experimental features on patient
 > outcomes.

- **Rank feature importance** using traditional statistical or machine
 > learning approaches.

- **Interpret findings** to understand the biological and clinical
 > significance of the key predictors.

### 3.3. Feature Importance & Model Evaluation

> **Feature Importance Assessment**:

- Cox model: Hazard ratios with confidence intervals.

- LASSO: Selected features with nonzero coefficients.

- RSF: Variable importance scores (permutation importance, SHAP
 > values).

> **Model Evaluation Metrics**:

- C-index: Measures model discrimination.

- Kaplan-Meier Curves: Visualizes risk groups.

- Time-dependent AUC: Evaluates predictive performance over time.

## 9. Conclusion

This project will provide the student with hands-on experience in
processing clinical data, survival modeling, statistics, and machine
learning (optional). The results will contribute to multi-omics data
integration for a deeper understanding of complex spatial TME (tumor
microenvironment) in ovarian cancer research.

During the period March - August 2025, the research assistant
contributed to a multimodal clinical modeling project on chemo-naive
ovarian cancer patients, under the supervision of Prof. Anniina Färkkilä
and mentoring support from a PhD student Ziqi Kang.

Her main responsibilities included data preprocessing, integration of
heterogeneous patient-level datasets, including clinical variables,
genomic features (e.g. copy number variation from whole-exome
sequencing), and spatial features derived from tCyCIF imaging data. She
worked with sensitive clinical and genomic data in secure computing
environments and followed established data management and
confidentiality protocols.

confidentiality protocols.

The research assistant implemented several survival and classification
modeling approaches, such as Cox proportional hazards models, penalized
regression models. She evaluated model performance using appropriate
statistical metrics and performed feature importance analyses to
identify key clinical and experimental predictors. In addition, she
interpreted modeling results in collaboration with the supervisors,
linking statistical findings to biological and clinical relevance. The
work culminated in a final report and presentation summarizing
methodological comparisons, key predictors, and insights into tumor
microenvironment associated patient heterogeneity.

## Slide 6
Feature selection pipeline
Data cleaning 
Merge different datasets
Combining features and selected them into the actual analysis
Cramer’s V for categorical variables
Functions for VIF, LASSO and Random Forest for automatized feature selection
Features chosen in two or more of these were chosen 
Recursive elimination with repeated cross-validation using C-index (survival outcome) for these selected features
Speaker notes: Cramer’s V measures the association between two categorical variables (in range 0 to 1) — basically checking how strongly they are related.

If Cramer’s V is very high, close to 1, it means two categorical variables overlap too much. In that case, including both wouldn’t add value, and could actually confuse the model.VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) detects multicollinearity, and I keep only those variables below a threshold (less than 5). That way, features that are too redundant are removed automatically.

LASSO is a regression method (statistical model). It assumes a linear relationship between features and outcome. It works by shrinking coefficients, and keeps only the variables that have a clear linear effect on survival.

Random Forest is a machine learning method and it doesn’t assume linearity. It can capture nonlinear relationships and interactions between features that LASSO might miss.

Using different kinds of methods makes the feature selection more reliable, because each method has different strengths.

Using different kinds of methods makes the feature selection more reliable, because each method has different strengths.

I then take the features that are selected by at least two of these three methods. This ensures robustness and it’s unlikely that two very different methods pick the same feature by chance.

Finally, I perform recursive feature elimination. That means the model is repeatedly trained to drop the least significant feature, and check model performance with repeated cross-validation. The metric I use is the C-index, which measures how well the model predicts survival.

## Slide 14
MOFA (multi-omics factor analysis) applicability tested
Identifies latent factors that explain variance across different data types
Speaker notes: MOFA can be viewed as a versatile and statistically rigorous generalization of principal component analysis to multi-omics data. 

Given several data matrices with measurements of multiple -omics data types on the same or on overlapping sets of samples, 
MOFA infers an interpretable low-dimensional representation in terms of a few latent factors. 

These learnt factors represent the driving sources of variation across data modalities, thus facilitating the identification of cellular states or disease subgroups.
